title.emphasis: 3. The Legal Consequence: A Service Relationship Under California Law

section.para: This dynamic—where a platform uses AI to exert creative control over user-generated IP—has profound implications under California's worker classification laws. The relationship, framed by the platform as a simple license-for-access, is vulnerable to re-characterization as a contract for services, triggering the state's retroactive "ABC test".5

listitem.para: Prong A (Control): The platform's moderation and algorithmic systems are the ultimate tools of control and direction. The argument that a user is "free from control" is severely weakened when their work is subject to constant, automated scrutiny and potential suppression based on the platform's subjective and commercial interests.1 The AI functions as an "algorithm as boss," dictating the "manner and means" of acceptable creative expression.5

Prong B (Course of Business): The platform's business is not merely providing hosting; it is curating and monetizing a stream of user-generated content. The creative labor of users is not outside the platform's usual course of business; it is the business.5

Prong C (Independent Business): This remains the most significant failure point. The platform's business model relies on the contributions of millions of casual "hobbyists," who are, by legal definition, not "customarily engaged in an independently established trade, occupation, or business".5

section.para: This legal analysis is further complicated by the influence of third parties. Payment processors, for example, often impose their own Acceptable Use Policies, creating another layer of content restriction that creators must adhere to in order to monetize their work. This functions as a form of indirect control, potentially implicating the payment processor as a joint employer under California's broad definition.5
